chunks.text: William G. Oldham is Professor Emeritus at UC Berkeley EECS. Contact information: 511 Cory Hall, tel: 510-642-2318, email: oldham@eecs.berkeley.edu. Office hours by email appointment at 509 Cory. Links include Personal Homepage, Publications, Dissertations, and Research Support. Research assistant: Blake Greenwood (bgreenwood@berkeley.edu).

William Oldham earned his B.S. (1960), M.S. (1961), and Ph.D. (1963) from Carnegie Institute of Technology. He worked at Siemens Research Laboratory in Germany before joining UC Berkeley's Electrical Engineering department in 1964. He served as Director of the Electronics Research Laboratory and Principal Investigator for the Joint Services Electronics Program, Director of California SEMATECH Center of Excellence, and Director of DARPA/SRC Research Network for Advanced Lithography. He held roles including Vice Chair of the Academic Senate, Chair of the Senate Ombudsperson Committee, and Secretary of the Berkeley Division Academic Senate.

William Oldham was elected National Science Foundation Senior Postdoctoral Fellow at Technical University Munich, Fellow of IEEE (1981), Guggenheim Fellow, and to the National Academy of Engineering (1986). He received the SRC Technical Excellence Award (1997), SIA University Research Award (2003), and IEEE Cledo Brunetti Award (2005). He has over 200 publications and 10 patents in semiconductor electronics.

Selected publications include works on maskless lithography, micromirrors, optical analysis, and semiconductor processes. Key publications: 'Design and fabrication of tilting and piston micromirrors for maskless lithography' (2005), 'Mirror-based pattern generation for maskless lithography' (2004), and 'Electrical Engineering: An Introduction' textbook (1984). Research focuses on lithography, microactuators, and semiconductor fabrication.

Selected honors and awards: IEEE Cledo Brunetti Award (2005), Berkeley Citation (2004), SIA University Research Award (2003), SIA University Leadership Award (2003), SRC Technical Excellence Award (1996), National Academy of Engineering Member (1986), Guggenheim Fellow (1985), IEEE Fellow (1981).
